topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my phone, a jarring intrusion into the quiet afternoon. My breath hitched, a sudden, tight constriction in my chest. It was a request from Mark. Mark, the architect of scraped knees and whispered insults from the third grade. My fingers hovered over the screen, trembling slightly before I finally tapped it open. His profile picture was the same smug, challenging grin I remembered so vividly. A wave of cold dread washed over me, making the air feel thin. I quickly minimized the app, shoving the phone into my pocket as if the screen itself might burn me. I started pacing the small room, my movements jerky and uneven. Every shadow seemed to stretch into something menacing, and I kept glancing towards the door, half-expecting him to materialize in the hallway.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen for what felt like an eternity. The little blue circle indicating a new connection pulsed mockingly. When I finally clicked, the sight of his name brought a sharp, unpleasant clenching in my stomach. I leaned back against the worn armchair, my hands gripping the fabric so tightly my knuckles turned white. A strange, hollow feeling settled over me, a sense of profound disorientation. What was he expecting? The silence of my apartment suddenly felt heavy, suffocating. I tried to rationalize it—a prank, a mistake—but the tremor in my hands betrayed my composure. I picked up a glass of water, the ice clinking too loudly in the quiet room, and took a long, deliberate sip, trying to steady the frantic rhythm of my pulse.

[story 3]

The sound of the phone vibrating was startling enough, but the content of the message—'Hey, long time no see'—sent a jolt straight through me. I dropped my backpack onto the floor with a muffled thud. My eyes scanned the room wildly, searching for an escape route that didn't exist. A flush crept up my neck, hot and undeniable. I couldn't bring myself to look at the text again; the words seemed amplified, echoing the old taunts I’d spent years trying to outrun. I stood frozen by the window, watching the street below, feeling a profound sense of vulnerability settle deep in my bones. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the strain in my molars, and I just stood there, rooted to the spot, unable to move or speak.
